questions.question: Explain the term RISC and state its primary design objective.
keyPoints: RISC stands for Reduced Instruction Set Computer
Primary objective is to simplify instructions so each executes in one clock cycle
Enables higher clock speeds and efficient pipelining
Reduces hardware complexity by moving complexity to compiler

questions.question: What is a Load-Store architecture in RISC processors? Explain why it is used.
keyPoints: Only LOAD and STORE instructions access memory
All arithmetic/logical operations work between registers only
Reduces memory bandwidth requirements
Simplifies the data path and enables faster execution
Allows overlapping memory operations with computation

questions.question: List and explain four key characteristics of RISC architecture.
keyPoints: Fixed-length instructions (typically 32 bits) - simplifies pipelining
Large register file (32+ registers) - reduces memory access
Load-store architecture - memory accessed only through load/store
Simple addressing modes - reduces decoder complexity
Single cycle execution - aims for CPI = 1

questions.question: Explain the five stages of a classic RISC pipeline with a diagram.
keyPoints: IF (Instruction Fetch): Fetch instruction from memory using PC
ID (Instruction Decode): Decode instruction, read registers
EX (Execute): ALU performs operation
MEM (Memory Access): Access memory for load/store if needed
WB (Write Back): Write result back to destination register

questions.question: Compare RISC and CISC architectures under these parameters: instruction complexity, addressing modes, and execution time.
keyPoints: RISC: Simple instructions, few addressing modes, single cycle; CISC: Complex instructions, multiple addressing modes, variable cycles
RISC: Fixed instruction length vs CISC variable length
RISC: More registers vs CISC fewer registers
RISC: Compiler does complexity vs CISC microcode handles complexity

questions.question: What is the role of the compiler in RISC design philosophy?
keyPoints: Compiler translates high-level code into simple RISC instructions
Compiler performs optimizations to combine simple instructions
Compiler manages register allocation
Complexity moved from hardware to software
Compiler must be sophisticated to generate efficient code

questions.question: Define CPI and explain why RISC aims for CPI close to 1.
keyPoints: CPI = Cycles Per Instruction - average clock cycles per instruction
RISC instructions are simple enough to execute in one cycle
Lower CPI means better performance
CPI of 1 means maximum efficiency in instruction execution
Achieved through simple instruction design and pipelining

questions.question: Explain in detail the RISC design philosophy including its origin, key principles, and advantages over CISC. Also mention any two RISC processors used in microcontrollers.
keyPoints: RISC originated in late 1970s-1980s at IBM, Stanford, Berkeley
Key principles: simple instructions, load-store, fixed-length, many registers, single cycle
Advantages: simpler hardware, faster clock, efficient pipelining, lower power
Comparison with CISC: RISC simpler, faster, more predictable timing
RISC processors in microcontrollers: ARM Cortex-M, MIPS, PIC32 (MIPS-based)
Modern significance: dominates mobile and embedded systems

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain instruction pipelining in RISC processors. Discuss hazards that can occur in pipeline and how they can be resolved.
keyPoints: 5-stage pipeline explained with diagram
IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB stages overlapping
Throughput increase: n instructions in n+4 cycles vs 5n cycles
Pipeline hazards: Structural (resource conflict), Data (dependency), Control (branch)
Solutions: Forwarding, Stall/NOP insertion, Branch prediction, Delayed branch

questions.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of RISC architecture. Why is RISC preferred for modern microcontrollers?
keyPoints: Advantages: Simpler hardware, higher clock speed, efficient pipelining, lower power consumption, better code predictability
Disadvantages: Larger code size, requires more memory, demands sophisticated compiler, more instructions for complex operations
RISC in microcontrollers: Low power (battery-powered devices), Small code size acceptable with flash memory, ARM dominance in embedded market, Cost-effective for high-volume production
Modern microcontroller examples: ARM Cortex-M0, M3, M4 series

questions.question: Explain the concept of delayed branching in RISC processors. How does it help in improving pipeline efficiency?
keyPoints: Delayed branch executes after the instruction following the branch
This instruction (delay slot) executes regardless of branch outcome
Keeps pipeline full during branch resolution
Compiler can fill delay slot with useful instruction
Reduces branch penalty without hardware complexity
Example: branch to X; instruction at PC+4 executes before branch to X
